aliases: 从高时间框架到低时间框架分析
从高时间框架到低时间框架的分析方法
从高时间框架向下分析
definition: A multi-timeframe analysis workflow that establishes directional bias on a higher timeframe (e.g., daily, weekly) using liquidity sweeps, swing points, and PD arrays, confirms structure and change in state of delivery on an intermediate timeframe, and executes entries on a lower timeframe (e.g., 5-minute, 1-minute) only when all timeframes align, targeting higher timeframe liquidity.
identification_rules: Begin on a higher timeframe (e.g., monthly, weekly, daily) to establish directional bias. Identify key PD arrays (FVG, Order Block, Breaker Block), swing highs/lows, internal/external range liquidity, and draw on liquidity targets.

Drop to an intermediate timeframe (e.g., 4-hour, hourly, 15-minute) to confirm the bias. Identify a change in state of delivery (CSD/CISD), market structure shift (MSS), or swing point that aligns with the higher timeframe direction, and mark key points of interest within this context.

Only proceed to a lower timeframe (e.g., 5-minute, 1-minute) when the intermediate timeframe shows price reacting at a point of interest or completing a CSD in the HTF direction. Wait for an entry trigger such as an order block, fair value gap, CISD, or fractal model (sweep + closure + continuation) consistent with the established bias.

Confirm that the lower timeframe setup targets the higher timeframe draw on liquidity (e.g., external range highs/lows, previous day highs/lows) and that all timeframes align in the same directional narrative.
Use the lower timeframe structure (e.g., protected swing, mean threshold) for stop placement, while basing the profit target on the higher timeframe liquidity objective.
The framework is fractal: the same logic applies across any chosen triplet (e.g., weekly/daily/4-hour, daily/4-hour/15-minute, hourly/15-minute/5-minute), adjusting the timeframe scale accordingly.

Standard timeframe pairing reference: weekly→4-hour (bias/structure), daily→1-hour, 4-hour→15-minute, 1-hour→5-minute, 15-minute→1-minute; the entry timeframe's parent is the structure layer, and the structure layer's parent is the bias layer.

Within the higher timeframe range, mark the equilibrium with 0.5 Fibonacci to distinguish premium (upper half) and discount (lower half); enter long at PD arrays in discount, short at PD arrays in premium.
Confirm the current session is within a Kill Zone (Asian 20:00-00:00, London 02:00-05:00, New York AM 08:30-11:00) to improve signal validity.
For OTE entries, overlay PD array confirmation within the 0.62-0.79 Fibonacci retracement zone of the displacement range before entering.
trading_implication: Top Down Analysis filters lower-timeframe noise by requiring alignment with higher timeframe bias, increasing the probability of expansion moves. It anchors entries to HTF draw on liquidity (e.g., previous day high/low, equal lows), allows tight stops on LTF protected swings or order blocks, and targets at least 2R toward HTF liquidity. The fractal nature lets traders flex the timeframe triplet, while skipping sessions where HTF liquidity is already taken or no LTF setup forms, improving risk-to-reward. Typical usage is around news events (e.g., 8:30/9:30 data releases) using time-driven multi-level price structure resonance.
common_mistakes: Entering on a lower timeframe setup without first confirming the higher timeframe bias and structural alignment.
Entering prematurely without a confirmed change in state of delivery (CSD/CISD) or swing point on the intermediate timeframe aligned with HTF bias.
Attempting to catch the wick on the HTF candle instead of waiting for the body/continuation formation.
Placing the profit target at a lower timeframe structure level rather than the higher timeframe draw on liquidity objective.
Anticipating a swing reversal on the HTF without a confirmed swing low/high (e.g., low with higher lows on each side).
Assuming that top-down alignment guarantees a winning trade—losses can still occur even with valid context.
Forcing a lower timeframe entry when no aligned setup forms, or entering every session without waiting for the HTF liquidity draw to be in play.
Ignoring the higher timeframe bearish/bullish structure and trading a lower timeframe reversal signal against the daily bias.
Treating an intermediate lower timeframe target (e.g., a swing low) as the final HTF draw on liquidity, failing to aim for the higher target.
Moving to the lower timeframe before the intermediate timeframe shows a confirmed reaction at a point of interest aligned with HTF bias.
Analyzing or trading solely on a single timeframe without incorporating higher timeframe context.
Attempting to trade a session when the HTF drawn liquidity has already been taken earlier in the session, leaving no valid target.
Jumping directly to lower timeframe without intermediate timeframe verification, leading to counter-trend trades or misalignment with overall market bias.
Over-relying on lower timeframe patterns while ignoring intermediate timeframe structure confirmation.
Failing to correctly mark higher timeframe liquidity targets, causing unreasonable lower timeframe target setting.
Over-trading on lower timeframe instead of waiting for key levels respected from higher timeframe.
Confusing key structures across timeframes, treating lower timeframe FVG as higher timeframe target.
Confusing single candle on higher timeframe with multiple candle sequence on lower timeframe, leading to Breaker Block identification errors.
Not using mean threshold correctly, simply using candle open or close as reaction zone.
Entering when HTF internal/external liquidity relationship is unclear.
Ignoring SMT divergences between correlated assets that may invalidate the bias.
Analyzing only one timeframe and missing critical context from higher or lower timeframes.
Entering outside Kill Zone when price touches OTE zone or PD array.
Entering too early after insufficient liquidity sweep, without clear sweep and displacement confirmation.
Forcing entry when HTF bias is not yet clear; wait for price to take one side of liquidity first.
Ignoring multiple nested ranges, mistaking local premium for larger discount, confusing PD arrays of different levels.
Relying solely on single timeframe OTE or Fibonacci zone without PD array overlap confirmation.
Using wrong timeframe pairing, e.g., entering on 5-minute with daily as structure layer, skipping 1-hour intermediate layer.
Rushing to find entry based only on HTF bias before structure timeframe completes CISD or market structure shift.
Forcing internal liquidity targets before HTF FVG actually forms; wait for FVG to form before marking.
Focusing on all steps of reversal sequence (Turtle Soup, Inversion, CISD, Breaker Block, FVG) simultaneously, causing analysis complexity; focus on one or two signals best suited.
Ignoring SMT cross-asset structure divergence signals, missing early confirmation of HTF bias.
Entering based on expected direction without Candle Two Closure or Candle Three Closure confirmation.
merge_notes: All 5 input cards are from ICT school; no school conflict. Definitions and rules were synthesized with emphasis on mechanism-level details (e.g., specific candle counts, pivot relationships). Some cards had fewer rules; union was taken. Nuances like Kill Zone usage and OTE entry were preserved from the most detailed card (The Foundation).
